WASHINGTON — Sen. Margaret Holloway (D-CA) called on Congress to pass comprehensive artificial intelligence regulation before the end of the year, warning that the technology's rapid advancement demands immediate action. "We cannot afford to wait another session while this technology reshapes every sector of our economy," Holloway said during a Senate Commerce Committee hearing on Thursday. "The companies building these systems," she added, "have repeatedly shown they will not regulate themselves." Her chief of staff, David Nakamura, told reporters the senator's office is circulating a draft bill among colleagues. "We've had productive conversations with members on both sides of the aisle, and we expect to have co-sponsors announced within two weeks," Nakamura said.

Orion Labs CEO Marcus Chen pushed back against the proposed regulatory framework during his testimony. "Innovation thrives when entrepreneurs have the freedom to experiment, and this bill would create a chilling effect on the entire AI startup ecosystem," Chen told the committee.

The hearing comes amid growing bipartisan concern over the use of AI in critical infrastructure, hiring decisions, and law enforcement surveillance. Holloway said her proposed framework would require companies to conduct independent safety audits before deploying high-risk AI systems and would establish a new federal office to monitor compliance.
